answer: Reaction center atoms: enolate carbon [CH2:10] attacks the α,β-unsaturated system [CH:20]=[CH:21]‑[C:22](=O) with the carbonyl oxygen [O:23]; the new C–C bond between [CH2:10] and [CH:20] closes the ring and the carbonyl becomes the enolate [O-:23] with Li+ as counterion.
Reaction center: the bond formation between phosphorus (P:4) and the azide nitrogen (N-:7), giving the cyclic phosphazine and neutralizing P.

The reaction center includes atoms [H:20], [CH:21], [C:22], [O:23], and [O:10]; H:20 transfers from the α-carbon [CH:21] to the tert-butoxide oxygen [O:10], producing the enolate with a C22=CH21 double bond, O23 carrying the negative charge, and O10 protonated to O+:10.

The reaction center is the carbon atom labeled [C:20], which undergoes nucleophilic addition from the alcohol to the carbonyl, converting from a double bond to [OH+:21] into a tetrahedral center bonded to both [OH:21] and the incoming [OH+:10].
The reaction center involves the carbonyl oxygen ([O:10]) being protonated by the hydronium hydrogen ([H:20]), with the hydronium oxygen ([OH2+:21]) donating that proton.
Reaction center involves mapped atoms [H:20], [C:21], [C:22], [O:23], and [N:10].
Reaction center: atoms 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 8, and 20—bonds 12–13 and 15–16 cleave, bond 13–14 becomes double, new bonds 12–15 and 8–20 form, and charge shifts to +1 on atom 14 and –1 on atom 16.

Reaction center: the hydrogen atom [H:20] shifts from the carbonyl oxygen [O:21] to the alcohol oxygen [OH:10], i.e., the bond between H:20 and O:21 breaks and a new bond between H:20 and OH:10 forms.
The reaction center involves the mapped N10–C20 bond and the hydroxyl at [OH2+:21]: the N10–C20 single bond becomes a double bond while the C20–O(H2O)+ bond breaks to release water.
